briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 5, 2026. Today, we will primarily cover news regarding the escalating climate crisis warnings both domestically and internationally, various adaptation efforts and carbon neutrality initiatives to address it, as well as progress and challenges in the transition to renewable energy.

sections.title: 🔴 Climate Crisis Warnings and Deepening Domestic and International Impacts
sections.content: Globally, the alarm bells of the climate crisis are ringing louder, and the likelihood of extreme weather events is increasing. A strong El Niño phenomenon has been forecast, leading to heatwave, drought, and flood emergencies in the Asian region, raising concerns that this could lead to record-breaking heatwaves next year [16, 22]. Domestically, the Gwangju region is observing longer summers and more frequent heatwaves, while agricultural production areas are experiencing heightened anxiety due to volatile climate change [3, 5, 23]. Notably, widespread ecosystem damage is being reported, including the death of 56% of the bee population. The northward movement of hail and the observation of marine heatwaves, often called 'ocean heatwaves,' suggest that climate change is altering the map of storms [8, 12, 25, 137]. Record-breaking May heatwaves and risks of drought and floods have also been reported in Ireland and Iowa, with even the Earth's rotation speed reaching a record high, underscoring the global spread of the climate crisis and the urgent need for action [133, 143, 147, 150, 155].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expansion of Domestic Climate Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Despite the threat of climate change, domestic efforts for adaptation and carbon neutrality practices are actively underway. In the agricultural sector, aT is working to establish climate-resilient farming practices, such as securing a year-round strawberry production base and distributing cultivation manuals for reducing vegetable pests and diseases in the Jeju region [1, 9, 10, 15]. Furthermore, Gwangju City is promoting climate adaptation policies to respond to longer summers and frequent heatwaves, while Cheongju City has developed a 'Disaster Information Map' that provides climate change and disaster information at a glance [5, 7, 19]. Across the country, various practical activities are being carried out to mark Environment Day, including carbon neutrality campaigns, volunteer recruitment, and educational outreach. Notably, Changwon City is encouraging citizen participation by offering double carbon neutrality points when using its 'Nubija' public bicycles [21, 26, 27, 28, 33, 34, 40, 42, 52, 54, 62, 79, 85, 90, 101]. Businesses are also participating in climate change response through eco-friendly technological innovation, such as the international success of carbon-neutral building certification and the expansion of SMR-powered ship development. Lotte Chilsung Beverage, for example, achieved a reduction of approximately 6,400 tons of greenhouse gas emissions last year [45, 46, 56, 59, 87, 88, 130].

sections.title: 🌱 Progress and Challenges in Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is accelerating both domestically and internationally, but it simultaneously faces significant challenges. The Export-Import Bank of Korea is contributing to economic security through stable energy supply and renewable energy transition, while Jeonbuk Institute for Regional Innovation is carrying out support projects for fostering renewable energy companies [111, 114]. Notably, Korea Zinc is actively pursuing eco-friendly businesses by securing grid connection approval for a super-large solar power and Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Australia, which is accelerating the development of energy storage facilities equivalent to 26,000 electric vehicles [105, 120, 121, 122, 124, 125, 128]. The Middle East region is also accelerating its renewable energy transition with investments totaling $100 billion, expanding opportunities for Korean companies to enter the market [126]. However, as seen in China's case, an inflexible power grid can lead to wasted renewable energy and a return to coal. Domestically, issues of delayed grid connection for renewable energy generation facilities are persistently raised, making the establishment of a stable power grid an urgent task [106, 109, 110, 116].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Policies and Key Discussions
sections.content: The international community continues its policy discussions and diverse approaches to addressing climate change. The 2026 Bonn Climate Change Conference is scheduled, and UN officials are urging accelerated renewable energy initiatives to address global energy vulnerability ahead of the COP31 Climate Change Conference [136, 139]. Observations by next-generation medical professionals on the impact of climate change on women's health and the necessity of establishing gender-equal policies are also being emphasized. Furthermore, the perspective that Iran, Israel, and the United States could form an alliance on climate change issues has been presented [13, 17, 140, 142]. Meanwhile, contrasting views on climate policy exist, with former U.S. President Donald Trump, for instance, misrepresenting climate change scenarios and announcing plans to support the coal industry with $700 million [132, 157]. Climate change is not merely a technical issue but a challenge that tests our capacity for cooperation. Amidst various environmental threats such as pollution, deforestation, and glacial melting, the importance of individual action is being reconfirmed through various discussions [146, 149, 154].

briefing.closing: As confirmed in today's briefing, climate change is emerging as a global threat, but simultaneously, various domestic and international efforts and technological advancements to resolve it are actively underway. We must build a better future through sustained climate action and policy cooperation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Trump uses wartime powers to dole out $700m to ‘clean, beautiful’ coal
articles.description: US president announces plans for two new coal plants, in Alaska and West Virginia, using Defense Production ActDonald Trump is using wartime presidential authority to hand $700m to coal-fired power plants in the US, the latest move by the president to bolster what he called “clean, beautiful coal”, despite it being the dirtiest of fossil fuels.“Today, we’re taking historic action to bring down the price of energy and the cost of living for all Americans with the power of clean, beautiful coal,”

summary.phenomenon: U.S. President Donald Trump announced plans to provide $700 million in aid to coal-fired power plants in the U.S., utilizing wartime presidential authority (Defense Production Act). This includes the construction of two new coal power plants in Alaska and West Virginia.
summary.cause: President Trump stated that these measures are being taken to bolster 'clean, beautiful coal' and to lower energy prices and the cost of living for all Americans.
summary.outlook: President Trump mentioned that this action would lower energy prices and the cost of living for all Americans. No other specific impacts or outlooks were discussed in the article.
